# Questions in Livestream 92

Live Stream #92: Ten Myths about the Good Doctor

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=532" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:52</a> | Why was Hatshepsut's memory attacked later in Thutmose III's reign instead of immediately? | He argued that Thutmose III had to wait out court factions still loyal to Hatshepsut before it became politically safe to move against her memory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:13</a> | Why did Egyptians start building mammisi shrines in the late period? | He tied them to continuity in royal cult and divine birth ideology as older kingship theology kept being reworked in later temple settings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=1181" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:41</a> | What evidence is there for the United Monarchy besides the Tel Dan Stele, and how big were the kingdoms of Saul, David, and Solomon? | He said Saul's kingdom was smaller, David's larger, and Solomon's larger still, with Solomon controlling important trade routes through the Levant even if exact borders remain debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=1411" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:31</a> | Does a horse-drawn cart meaning for "chariot" in Genesis 41 remove the problem of Joseph arriving in Egypt in the 19th century BC? | No. He said Mesopotamian evidence for early chariots does not show Egyptian chariotry in the 19th century BC; Egyptian evidence appears later, especially with Hyksos-period horse breeding. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=1700" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:20</a> | Is there a minimalist equivalent to *On the Reliability of the Old Testament*, and where is a good steel-man case for biblical minimalism? | He pointed to Israel Finkelstein and Neil Asher Silberman's *The Bible Unearthed* as the strongest representative work, while saying he does not think it withstands scrutiny. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=1810" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:10</a> | Were the borders of the promised land Moses could not enter defined by Genesis 10:19 or Numbers 34:4? | He said Numbers 34 is more expansive, but the key point is that Moses was excluded from the land's blessing and fruitfulness, not merely from touching a boundary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=2860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:40</a> | Why do many scholars deny that Canaanites sacrificed children, and is Gezer the nail in the coffin? | He said some denial is ideological, and argued that Egyptian reliefs of Canaanite child sacrifice are stronger evidence than Gezer alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=3162" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:42</a> | Is there any evidence for the historical Moses? | He said there is no direct evidence, but Moses' Egyptian name and parallels with Egyptian protest-mediator patterns are meaningful indirect evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=3626" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:26</a> | Could either Reuel or Jethro have been a title? | He said they could be titles or names, and they may even refer to the same person, but the text does not give enough information to decide firmly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=3813" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:33</a> | Does the word translated "birthing stool" in Exodus 1:16 most likely mean a potter's wheel and refer to Khnum? | No. He said Exodus 1:16 refers to birthing bricks, while the Khnum potter's-wheel imagery belongs more naturally with creation imagery in Genesis 1-2. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=4069" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:49</a> | Is Kitchen right that Kushan recurs as a place name and that Kushu is synonymous with Edom? | He said he loves Kitchen's work but thinks this particular claim is probably wrong; he does not think Kushan refers to Edom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=4177" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:37</a> | Is the Judges pattern of Israel's sin, punishment, repentance, and deliverance also found in Egyptian literature such as the draftsman Nebre text? | He said Kitchen makes a plausible case, especially with the Nebre and Nakhtamun example of wrongdoing, sickness, petition, and divine deliverance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=4384" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:04</a> | How does Christ's Davidic lineage interact with the virgin birth? | He argued that Mary was herself in David's line, so Christ did not need Davidic DNA supplied separately; how the Holy Spirit completed the genetic complement is not explained. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=4864" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:04</a> | What is his favorite book on the composition of the Pentateuch? | He said no book has fully satisfied him; his own view is that Moses wrote core documents later collated and updated by editors, not ideologically redacted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=5224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:04</a> | Will there be a David Falk guided tour of Egypt anytime soon? | He said he would love to do one, but a prior tour did not get enough sign-ups; supporters would need to organize enough interest with the tour company. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=5632" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:52</a> | Are the prophets just crisis managers and social analysts? | He said they do perform crisis management and social analysis, but not merely that; they interpret Israel's crisis through covenant faithfulness and God's purposes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=5853" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:33</a> | What is the basis for post-exilic dating of the Deuteronomistic books, and is that framework consistent with archaeological data? | He distinguished the books rather than treating them as one block, placing Kings as a post-exilic compilation while being less certain about Samuel, Joshua, and Ruth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=6132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:12</a> | Did Second Temple interest in angels, demons, and giants influence the Septuagint and affirm a late dating of Daniel? | He said such themes have older Near Eastern antecedents, so they might influence translation choices without proving a late date for Daniel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=6465" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:45</a> | Do we know who Pharaoh Necho of 2 Kings 23:29 was, and do we have a historical date for him? | Yes. He identified him as Necho II of the 26th Dynasty, dated roughly 610-595 BC, known especially through Assyrian records. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=6834" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:54</a> | When will he respond to a theological response to his popcorn review posted on MythVision? | He gave a joking noncommittal answer, saying he may or may not respond. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=6883" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:43</a> | Is O'Callahan's identification of 7Q5 as Mark 6:52-53 possible? | He said he had not looked at 7Q5 or O'Callahan's argument, so he could not answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=6978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:18</a> | Is he familiar with scholarship on original monotheism, such as Winfried Corduan, and does he think it is plausible? | He said he had not read Corduan and did not know the view well enough at that point to comment responsibly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=7087" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:07</a> | How was time kept in Israel around Jesus' time, and did they use water clocks or 12 hours of day and night? | He said the 12-hour day and 12-hour night are clear, but whether Israelites used water clocks, sundials, or both is uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=7317" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:57</a> | What are his thoughts on connections between Judaism and Zoroastrianism? | He said connections appear in post-exilic works, but cautioned against projecting them into pre-exilic texts or assuming plagiarism instead of intertextuality. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=7713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:33</a> | Do skeptics give alternative explanations for Exodus evidence such as the abandonment of Avaris, changing foreign remains in Canaan, and Egyptian loanwords? | Yes. He said skeptics have individual alternate explanations, but he argues the cumulative alternatives create a high cognitive load compared with the Exodus explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=8035" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:55</a> | When does he think Genesis was written? | He placed Genesis in the 13th century BC, contemporaneous with the rest of the Pentateuch's core documents, citing retrojected terms as evidence for Mosaic-era composition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=8419" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:19</a> | How should Catholics and Orthodox be engaged on Second Temple literature versus the apostolic fathers? | He said he usually does not pit them against each other, since the fathers themselves already lean heavily on the Old Testament world the Second Temple material helps illuminate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=8805" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:45</a> | When will there be a Christian renaissance in Old Testament reliability studies? | He said it may still be years or decades away, but thinks scholarship has already moved substantially from where it was forty years earlier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=9162" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:42</a> | Why do yods and vavs get swapped in some Hebrew words, such as Kish for Kush? | He tied this to the standardization of Hebrew, matres lectionis, changing vocalization, and the later loss of original pronunciation contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=9454" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:34</a> | What does he think of recent RTI results on the Moabite Stone that seem to confirm the reading "House of David"? | He strongly praised RTI technology and said the reading makes sense; he would like RTI scans used more widely for inscriptions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=9725" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:05</a> | What is his opinion of the bowl with a year 16 date for Horemheb that Redford studied and that later reappeared in a private collection? | He was not ready to extend Horemheb's reign past about year 15, suggesting the bowl could be commemorative or tied to later Theban dating practices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=10453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:13</a> | How was the mixed multitude integrated into Israel? | He said they were attached to tribes for movement and organization, then gradually integrated through intermarriage, shared wilderness life, and assimilation into Israelite identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=10911" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:51</a> | Which Egyptian pharaoh was the biggest or most extreme, depending on the category? | He said Ramses II had the biggest ego, Thutmose III was the greatest or baddest Pharaoh, Khufu spent the most, and one of the Ptolemies was likely the biggest drunkard. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=11148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:48</a> | What are his thoughts on the Annabelle doll? | He said he thinks it is just a doll, regards much of Ed and Lorraine Warren's work skeptically, and sees many claims around Annabelle as coincidence or methodological overreach. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=12111" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:51</a> | Did the serpent Moses raised come from Amorite practice? | He thought it more likely came from Egyptian symbolism, especially the raised uraeus, which Moses repurposed in a Yahweh-worship context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=12290" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:50</a> | Is it unbiblical to use violence in defense or war against evil? | He said self-defense against someone trying to kill you is not murder, but cautioned against the broad phrase "war against evil" because it muddies the issue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=13244" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:44</a> | If original monotheism means monotheism is independently common in less developed cultures and may be more ancient than often thought, is that plausible? | He said that clarified form has some plausibility, especially if early local cults were monotheistic or henotheistic before larger polytheistic systems developed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=13472" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:32</a> | In Proverbs 7, does the warning about adultery also point to spiritual adultery? | Yes. He said the immediate context is sexual sin, but the passage readily extends to covenant unfaithfulness toward God as a secondary spiritual meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=14022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:42</a> | How would he respond to the claim that the Canaanite conquest was immoral? | He said modern moral assumptions shape the objection, and he followed John Walton in emphasizing "drive out" language rather than reading the conquest as simple total ethnic cleansing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/EsTdqeuDvS4?t=14376" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:36</a> | As an Egyptologist, a Canadian, and a sci-fi fan, what does he think of *Stargate*, especially SG-1? | He said he likes *Stargate* and loved the original film, but Babylon 5 remains his favorite science-fiction series. |
